params.text:
Three words that mean different things

People often use these terms loosely, but they describe different events. Knowing the difference helps you report clearly.

  • An accident is an unplanned event that did cause harm or damage — for example, a person falls and fractures a wrist.
  • An incident is any event that harmed, or could have harmed, a person, staff member or the service — including violence, medication errors and safeguarding concerns.
  • A near miss is an event that could have caused harm but, by luck or quick action, did not.

Tap the cards below to check your understanding.

params.alt: Illustration for Accident, incident, near miss

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Match each event to the right category.

dialogs.text: A resident trips on a loose rug and grazes their arm, needing a dressing.
dialogs.answer: Accident – an unplanned event that caused actual harm. Record it and act to remove the hazard (the loose rug).

dialogs.text: A carer notices a rug is loose but no one has tripped on it yet.
dialogs.answer: Near miss – harm was possible but did not happen this time. Report it so the rug is fixed before anyone falls.

dialogs.text: A staff member is verbally threatened by a visitor.
dialogs.answer: Incident – an event affecting someone's safety or wellbeing, even where no physical injury occurred.

dialogs.text: A medicine is prepared for the wrong person but spotted and stopped before it is given.
dialogs.answer: Near miss – a medication error that was caught in time. Reporting it helps improve the medicines process.

dialogs.text: A hoist sling is found to be frayed during a check.
dialogs.answer: Near miss / hazard – the equipment could have failed under load. Take it out of use and report it at once.

dialogs.text: A person is given a double dose of medication and becomes unwell.
dialogs.answer: Accident / incident – actual harm resulted. It must be recorded, may need medical attention, and may trigger further duties.
